talk_title: STK-12 IL-2 agonist plus pembrolizumab and chemotherapy in PD-L1 negative NSCLC

biomarkers.name: PD-L1 negative
biomarkers.finding: 89% of enrolled patients were PD-L1 negative

biomarkers.name: STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation
biomarkers.finding: ORR 50% in this subgroup, median PFS 5.5 months

models.name: Phase I STK-12 + pembrolizumab + carboplatin
models.genotype_or_key_features: Stage 4 non-squamous NSCLC, 89% PD-L1 negative, enriched STK11/KEAP1/SMARCA4 alterations

MOA_summary: STK-12 is a recombinant IL-2 partial agonist that selectively expands antigen-experienced CD8+ T cells while sparing NK and Treg cells, enhancing IFNγ and IP-10 production and clonal TCR expansion, thereby improving response rates when combined with pembrolizumab and chemotherapy in PD-L1 negative NSCLC.
key_findings: Overall response rate of 50% and disease control rate of 97% in a high‑risk PD‑L1 negative NSCLC cohort (n=39).
In STK11/KEAP1/SMARCA4 altered subgroup, ORR increased to 61% with 100% disease control.
STK‑12 induced robust expansion of 4‑1BB+ CD8 T cells and >10‑fold clonal expansion of pre‑existing TCR clones.
Minimal classic IL‑2 toxicities observed; most common AE was reversible maculopapular rash in ~50% of patients.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: STK‑12 did not expand NK cells or Tregs, preserving a favorable safety profile.
Efficacy appears limited in patients lacking pre‑existing antigen‑experienced T cells, suggesting dependence on recall responses.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade provides antigen exposure while STK‑12 expands effector T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression with ORR 50% vs historical 23‑32%

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy induces immunogenic cell death, increasing antigen availability for STK‑12‑expanded T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Manageable safety profile, no DLTs observed

open_questions: Does STK‑12 generate de‑novo neoantigen responses or rely solely on pre‑existing clones?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to maximize T‑cell expansion while minimizing rash?

talk_title: Tertiary lymphoid structures predict response to durvalumab, trastuzumab, pertuzumab in HER2‑enriched early breast cancer

biomarkers.name: TLS-associated niche fraction
biomarkers.finding: Higher niche fraction correlated with PCR (pCR) after DTP therapy

biomarkers.name: TIL percentage
biomarkers.finding: Patients achieving PCR had higher baseline TILs and greater increase post‑treatment

MOA_summary: Durvalumab combined with HER2‑targeted antibodies induces formation of tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) composed of Tfh‑like CD4+ cells, mature dendritic cells, and proliferating B cells; these TLS niches correlate with enhanced CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and higher pathologic complete response in HER2‑enriched breast cancer.
key_findings: TLS‑associated immune module (Tfh‑like CD4+, LAMB3+ mature DCs, cycling B cells) is enriched in pretreatment tumors of patients achieving PCR.
Spatial analysis shows co‑localization of these cell types within 20‑µm niches, defining TLS structures.
Higher TLS niche fraction predicts PCR (p<0.01) and correlates with increased intratumoral CD8+ T cells post‑treatment.
CellChat indicates CD4+ Tfh cells activate B cells via IL‑2, CD40, and ICAM pathways within TLS.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High‑endothelial venules and specific dendritic subsets (XCR1+) were not fully characterized, suggesting heterogeneity in TLS composition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade releases PD‑1 inhibition, allowing TLS‑primed T cells to act.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic increase in PCR rate to 49% vs historical <30% with HER2‑targeted therapy alone

open_questions: Do post‑treatment biopsies show expansion of TLS structures, confirming dynamic induction?
Can specific TLS‑associated taxa be modulated to further improve response?

talk_title: Inhibiting nonsense‑mediated decay (SMG1) to enhance antitumor immunity

biomarkers.name: PD‑1+ CD8 T cells
biomarkers.finding: Increased frequency after SMG1 inhibition in patient‑derived tumor fragments

models.name: Various CRC and lung cancer lines

MOA_summary: SMG1 inhibition blocks nonsense‑mediated decay, stabilizing frameshift‑derived PTC transcripts that encode highly immunogenic neoantigens; these are presented on MHC‑I, leading to CD8+ T‑cell activation and tumor control, synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
key_findings: SMG1 inhibition increased PD‑1+ CD8 T‑cell activation in ex vivo tumor fragments without affecting adjacent normal tissue.
In Lewis lung carcinoma, SMG1 inhibitor monotherapy matched anti‑PD‑1 efficacy; combination yielded superior tumor control.
Immunopeptidomics identified six neoantigen peptides up‑regulated only after NMD inhibition; four were immunogenic in vitro.
MHC‑I blockade abolished T‑cell activation induced by SMG1 inhibition, confirming antigen‑dependent mechanism.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SMG1 inhibition showed minimal toxicity in normal tissue ex vivo and no significant weight loss in mice, suggesting tumor selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade may unleash T cells primed by newly presented NMD‑derived neoantigens.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Superior tumor control versus either monotherapy in mouse model

open_questions: Will chronic SMG1 inhibition lead to autoimmunity due to presentation of self‑derived PTC peptides?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to maximize tumor antigen presentation while minimizing potential off‑target effects?

talk_title: High‑fiber dietary intervention improves immunotherapy outcomes in melanoma

biomarkers.name: Bacteroides spp.
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in high‑fiber arm and associated with response

biomarkers.name: Secondary/primary bile acid ratio
biomarkers.finding: Decreased ratio in high‑fiber arm correlates with improved outcomes

biomarkers.name: Classical monocytes (immunosuppressive cluster)
biomarkers.finding: Reduced frequency over time in high‑fiber arm

models.name: Randomized double‑blind high‑fiber vs control diet with ICB
models.genotype_or_key_features: Metastatic and neoadjuvant melanoma patients receiving standard checkpoint blockade

clinical_data.value: Numerically higher in high‑fiber arm (exact % not disclosed)

MOA_summary: A high‑fiber, whole‑food diet reshapes the gut microbiome toward response‑associated taxa, lowers secondary/primary bile acid ratios, reduces immunosuppressive classical monocytes, and enhances intratumoral CD8+ T‑cell and cytotoxic lymphocyte infiltration, thereby improving checkpoint inhibitor efficacy in melanoma.
key_findings: High‑fiber diet did not alter overall alpha/beta diversity but increased patient‑specific enrichment of known response‑associated taxa.
Serum bile acid profiling showed a significant decrease in secondary/primary bile acid ratio in the high‑fiber arm.
Classical monocyte immunosuppressive cluster decreased over time only in high‑fiber patients.
Digital spatial profiling revealed greater CD8+ T‑cell and cytotoxic lymphocyte density post‑intervention in responders receiving high‑fiber diet.

combination_strategies.rationale: Diet‑induced microbiome and metabolic changes prime the TME for ICB response.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rates and lower cutaneous toxicity

open_questions: Would isolated fermentable fiber supplements recapitulate the benefits of whole‑food high‑fiber diets?
What are the long‑term durability of immune benefits after cessation of the diet?

talk_title: IL‑17‑producing TH17 cells drive neurotoxicity in immune‑related adverse events of the CNS

biomarkers.name: POLR6+ CD4+ PD‑1+ Tfh‑like cells
biomarkers.finding: Higher burden correlates with increased neuroinflammation on PET and worse cognitive scores

biomarkers.name: IL‑17 in CSF
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in IRAE‑CNS and reduced after anti‑IL‑17 therapy

models.name: Multi‑center real‑world IRAE‑CNS cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Various solid tumors treated with ICIs; excluded brain metastases

MOA_summary: A POLR6‑positive CD4+ PD‑1+ TH17‑like cell subset expands in the CSF of ICI‑treated patients, secreting IL‑17 and other inflammatory mediators that drive neuroinflammation; blockade of IL‑17A mitigates PET‑detected inflammation and improves neurologic outcomes.
key_findings: Identified a disease‑specific POLR6+ CD4+ PD‑1+ TH17‑like cell cluster producing IL‑17, MCP1, CCL11, granzyme B, etc.
Higher POLR6+ cell burden correlated with greater PET‑measured neuroinflammation and worse cognitive scores.
Clonal T‑cell expansions persisted up to 12 months, indicating ongoing antigen‑driven response, with NMDA‑R1 as a dominant neuroantigen.
Retrospective anti‑IL‑17A treatment was associated with reduced neuroinflammation and improved neurologic function without affecting cancer outcomes.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target downstream cytokine of pathogenic TH17 cells to reduce CNS toxicity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced PET signal and improved cognition in treated patients

open_questions: What is the source of NMDA‑R1 antigen driving TH17 expansion?
Can prospective anti‑IL‑17A therapy prevent IRAE‑CNS when given prophylactically?

talk_title: NAT10‑MYC loop drives autophagy‑dependent MHC‑I downregulation and immunotherapy resistance in MSS colorectal cancer

biomarkers.name: NAT10 expression
biomarkers.finding: High NAT10 correlates with MSS status, low immune score, and poor survival

biomarkers.name: MHC‑I surface expression
biomarkers.finding: Increases after NAT10 knockout or inhibition

models.name: Syngeneic CRC tumor in immunocompetent mice
Fudan and TMDU CRC cohorts
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patient tumors with NAT10 expression data

MOA_summary: NAT10 acetylates RNA (ac4C) stabilizing autophagy genes (ATG3, ATG5, Beclin‑1), leading to increased autophagic flux that degrades MHC‑I molecules; a MYC‑NAT10 positive feedback loop sustains this program, causing immune evasion in MSS CRC, which can be reversed by NAT10 inhibition to restore MHC‑I and sensitize tumors to anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
key_findings: NAT10 is overexpressed in MSS CRC and predicts worse overall survival.
NAT10 knockout increases CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and tumor regression in mice.
Combination of NAT10 inhibition with anti‑PD‑1 yields complete tumor regression in 80% of mice.
MYC transcriptionally up‑regulates NAT10; NAT10‑mediated ac4C stabilizes MYC mRNA, forming a feed‑forward loop.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NAT10 inhibition may affect normal cells due to global RNA modification, but preclinical data show limited toxicity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restored MHC‑I expression after NAT10 inhibition enables effective checkpoint blockade.

open_questions: Will NAT10 inhibition synergize with other immunotherapies (e.g., CTLA‑4 blockade) in MSS CRC?
What biomarkers best predict response to NAT10‑targeted therapy in patients?

talk_title: Versican proteolysis restores tumor matrix signaling to reverse checkpoint resistance

biomarkers.name: VCAN proteolysis level
biomarkers.finding: High VCAN proteolysis correlates with increased CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and better response to pembrolizumab in colorectal cancer trial

MOA_summary: Proteolytic cleavage of versican generates bioactive fragments that promote formation of immunotriads (CD4+ Tfh‑like cells, CD8+ T cells, dendritic cells) within the tumor stroma, enhancing CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and synergizing with checkpoint blockade to overcome immune exclusion.
key_findings: Versican overexpression in MC38 tumors induced immunotriad formation and cured >70% of mice with anti‑PD‑1 alone.
In VCAN‑high LSC tumors, versican plus dual checkpoint blockade (PD‑1 + CTLA‑4) was required for tumor control.
Exogenous versican delivered via LNP mRNA or recombinant protein showed monotherapy activity in VCAN‑low models and synergized with ICB.
Efficacy depended on cDC1 dendritic cells and CD8+ T cells; depletion abrogated therapeutic benefit.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential concern that matrix remodeling could facilitate tumor cell dissemination; however, versican fragments appear to act primarily on immune cell recruitment rather than tumor invasion.

combination_strategies.rationale: Versican‑induced TLS‑like structures prime T cells for checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Monotherapy effect in VCAN‑low tumors; enhanced cure rates with PD‑1 alone

combination_strategies.rationale: VCAN‑high tumors require broader checkpoint inhibition to overcome deep immune exclusion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in LSC model

open_questions: Which tumor types beyond CRC and lung cancer exhibit VCAN proteolysis deficiency and could benefit from versican therapy?
What is the optimal dosing schedule for versican protein versus mRNA delivery to maximize immune activation while minimizing potential pro‑metastatic effects?
